entries.title: HTTP/2 in Burp: overview
entries.body:
# HTTP/2 support in Burp

Burp works fully with HTTP/2. By default, if a server advertises HTTP/2 support via ALPN during the TLS handshake, Burp communicates with it over HTTP/2 - this is both faster and more accurately reflects the real behavior of modern applications.

You can work with HTTP/2 messages in Burp in two ways:

- **HTTP/1-style representation in the message editor.** Burp shows an HTTP/2 request in the familiar HTTP/1 text form. You edit as usual, and Burp NORMALIZES your edits and sends an equivalent, correct HTTP/2 request. This is convenient for 99% of ordinary work.
- **The exact HTTP/2 view via the Inspector.** When you need control over the raw structure (pseudo-headers, special characters in names), you use the **Inspector** - it shows the request as it will actually go out over HTTP/2 and lets you build what can't be expressed in HTTP/1 at all. This is the basis for HTTP/2-exclusive attacks.

Where HTTP/2 is supported: **Proxy**, **Repeater**, **Logger**, the scanner, and others. There's a nuance with **Intruder** - it works with ordinary HTTP/2 requests, but does NOT do "kettled" requests (ones that can't be represented in HTTP/1).

## The main setting

**Settings > Network > HTTP > Default to HTTP/2 if the server supports it** - globally controls the behavior:

- enabled (by default) - Burp uses HTTP/2 by default everywhere the server supports it;
- disabled - HTTP/1 is used by default, but HTTP/2 can be enabled manually for a specific request via the protocol toggle in the **Inspector**.

```
Settings > Network > HTTP
  [v] Default to HTTP/2 if the server supports it

In Repeater, switch the protocol of a specific request:
  Inspector -> Request attributes -> Protocol: HTTP/1 <-> HTTP/2
```

> Tip: leave HTTP/2 enabled by default - that way you test the application in the same protocol that real users and browsers use with it. Many modern bugs (especially desync) live exactly at the seam of an HTTP/2 frontend and an HTTP/1 backend.

Next - three topics: HTTP/2 basics for a Burp user, normalization in the editor, and carrying out HTTP/2-exclusive attacks.

entries.title: HTTP/2 basics for a Burp user
entries.body:
# HTTP/2 basics for a Burp user

To understand HTTP/2 attacks in Burp, you need to know how HTTP/2 differs from HTTP/1 at the format level. The differences aren't cosmetic - a whole class of vulnerabilities grows out of exactly them.

## A binary protocol instead of a text one

HTTP/1 is text-based: headers are separated by line breaks, name and value by a colon. HTTP/2 is BINARY: data is transmitted in frames, and the service characters (line break, colon) no longer work as separators. You can stuff them right inside a header name or value - and for the protocol itself this won't break the structure.

This is exactly the foundation of HTTP/2-exclusive attacks: what's impossible in HTTP/1 (there a line break would immediately terminate the header) is quite transmittable in HTTP/2 as part of the data.

## Pseudo-headers instead of the request line

In HTTP/1 the first line is `GET /path HTTP/1.1`. In HTTP/2 there's no request line; instead there are five pseudo-headers (they start with a colon):

- `:method` - the method (GET, POST, ...);
- `:path` - the path together with the query string;
- `:authority` - the analog of the `Host` header;
- `:scheme` - the scheme (http/https);
- `:status` - the response code (in responses only).

By the specification, pseudo-headers must come BEFORE ordinary headers.

## Frames and an explicit length

A message is transmitted in separate frames with an explicit length field. This removes the classic HTTP/1 ambiguity around `Content-Length` and parsing message boundaries - the length is known exactly, not inferred from the text.

## Header names - in lowercase

By the HTTP/2 specification, header names must be in lowercase. Uppercase letters can technically get through, but they violate the spec and some servers reject them.

```
HTTP/1:                          HTTP/2 (logically):
GET /search?q=1 HTTP/1.1         :method  GET
Host: example.com               :path    /search?q=1
User-Agent: ...                 :authority example.com
                                :scheme  https
                                user-agent ...
```

## How Burp shows this

Burp displays an HTTP/2 request in a HUMAN-READABLE HTTP/1 form (with a request line and headers), while under the hood it keeps a correct HTTP/2. At that:

- the **Inspector** shows the real pseudo-headers (`:method`, `:path`, `:authority`, `:scheme`) as separate fields;
- in the **Repeater** you can send HTTP/2 requests directly;
- the dual representation lets you test HTTP/2-specific vectors without fiddling with raw frames by hand.

> Tip: for ordinary work you don't need to think about frames and pseudo-headers at all - edit the request like a familiar HTTP/1, and Burp will assemble a valid HTTP/2 itself. You need to get into the **Inspector** to the pseudo-headers only when you SPECIFICALLY want to build what HTTP/1 doesn't express (for attacks).

entries.title: HTTP/2 normalization in the message editor
entries.body:
# HTTP/2 normalization in the message editor

Burp shows an HTTP/2 request in HTTP/1 syntax - that's more convenient to edit. But HTTP/1 and HTTP/2 are built differently, so, when turning your text back into HTTP/2 before sending, Burp NORMALIZES it - brings it to a form correct by the HTTP/2 specification. Usually this is a boon: you write the familiar way, and valid HTTP/2 goes out on the wire. But sometimes normalization gets in the way - and then you have to bypass it.

## The HTTP/1 -> HTTP/2 mapping

Burp maps the elements:

- `:method` and `:path` are assembled from the request line (`GET /path HTTP/1.1`);
- `:authority` is taken from the `Host` header;
- the remaining headers are carried over as is.

## What exactly gets normalized

When your HTTP/1 text is converted to HTTP/2, Burp automatically:

- **lowercases all header names** (any uppercase letters become lowercase - this is an HTTP/2 spec requirement);
- **removes the `Connection` header** (it's specific to HTTP/1 and forbidden in HTTP/2);
- **puts `Host` in the right place**, since it maps to `:authority`, and pseudo-headers must come before ordinary ones.

The point: as long as your input is syntactically correct for HTTP/1, the output will be correct HTTP/2.

```
You wrote:                       Burp will send over HTTP/2:
Connection: keep-alive   ----->  (removed)
User-Agent: x            ----->  user-agent: x      (lowercase)
Host: example.com        ----->  :authority: example.com (to the start)
```

## When normalization gets in the way

If you're testing how the server reacts to DELIBERATELY malformed HTTP/2 (uppercase letters in a name, a colon or line break inside a header, a forbidden `Connection`), normalization will "fix" all of this and ruin your attack. Then the message editor won't do - you need to build the request exactly.

## How to bypass normalization

To send knowingly invalid HTTP/2, use the **Inspector** instead of the text editor. The **Inspector** gives direct control over header names and values (separate **Name**/**Value** fields) and does not bring them to the specification - what you enter is what goes out.

The only exception is the `Connection` header: it's cut out by default even from the **Inspector**. This behavior can be changed via the **Repeater** menu in its HTTP/2 settings.

```
Ordinary editing  -> message editor (normalization ON, convenient)
Malformed HTTP/2  -> Inspector (exact control, no normalization)
Send Connection   -> Repeater menu > HTTP/2 options
```

> Pitfall: if your HTTP/2 attack "doesn't fire", the first suspect is that normalization ate the vector. Uppercase letters, extra colons, `Connection` - the editor quietly fixes all of this. Build such requests only through the **Inspector** and verify in it that exactly what you intended is being sent.

entries.title: HTTP/2-exclusive attacks
entries.body:
# Carrying out HTTP/2-exclusive attacks

Burp's main feature on the HTTP/2 front is the ability to send requests that CANNOT BE EXPRESSED in HTTP/1. This opens a class of attacks unavailable to other tools: HTTP/2-specific desync and request smuggling, injections into header names and values via a line break, manipulations with the method and path.

## Why this is exclusive

In HTTP/1 a line break immediately terminates a header, and a colon separates name and value - nothing extraneous can be inserted there. In HTTP/2 (binary) these characters are NOT service characters inside data, so you can stuff them right into a header name or value. The spec says to reject such requests, but some servers tolerate them - and this is where exploitable states arise (frontend and backend desync).

## Working through the Inspector

Such requests are built in the **Inspector** - it shows each header and pseudo-header as separate **Name** and **Value** fields, close to the real HTTP/2 structure. Most values are edited with a double click right in the main list.

What you can do (per the documentation):

- **inject colons into header names**;
- **arbitrary spaces or line breaks inside the method and path** (`:method` / `:path`);
- **line breaks anywhere** in a header name or value.

## How to insert a line break (\r\n) into a header

1. Click the chevron to expand the header entry.
2. Place yourself in the **Name** or **Value** field.
3. Press **Shift + Return** - a `\r\n` sequence will be inserted.

```
Inspector (Repeater) -> expand the header (chevron)
  Name:  [ foo            ]
  Value: [ bar            ]  <- Shift+Return here inserts \r\n
```

## Kettled requests

As soon as you apply such edits, the request becomes **kettled** - it can no longer be shown exactly in HTTP/1 syntax, because the text editor would lose some of the information during conversion. So kettled requests exist only in the HTTP/2 representation.

The practical consequence: kettled requests are handled by the **Repeater** and the scanner, but the **Intruder** does NOT support them - for fuzzing over a kettled vector the Intruder won't do.

## What this is used to find

The typical targets of HTTP/2-exclusive attacks are request smuggling/desync at the "HTTP/2 frontend -> HTTP/1 backend" seam: H2.CL and H2.TE desyncs, request tunnelling, response queue poisoning, bypassing frontend restrictions through the injection of hidden headers. Burp Scanner can find some of these problems automatically, but for confirmation and fine-tuning the manual **Inspector** + **Repeater** is more convenient.

> Pitfall: by the specification, these injections SHOULD be rejected - and on a correctly configured server they are rejected. They fire only where a server/proxy violates the spec and lets the "dirt" through. So a negative result is the norm; what's interesting is exactly those targets where a spec-pinned server suddenly behaves abnormally.

> Tip: remember the connection with normalization. So that the vector isn't eaten, build the attack strictly in the **Inspector** and make sure exactly what you entered is being sent. The `Connection` header, if it's needed for the attack, is allowed separately via the **Repeater > HTTP/2 options** menu.
